questions.prompt: By the time an Incident Report reaches you, what has the SOC analyst already done?
choices: Triaged the signal, classified it, and written a recommendation.

choices.label: Run a vulnerability scan on the affected host.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the model. Vulnerability scanning is a different product class and not part of the SOC's pre-handoff work.

choices.label: Notified the customer directly.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Customer-facing comms are the MSP's job. The Incident Report comes to the MSP first.

choices.label: Patched the underlying vulnerability.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The SOC analyses and recommends; remediation is the actioning party's job.

questions.explanation: The Incident Report is the output of analysis, not its input. Triage, classification, and recommendation have all happened before you see it.

questions.prompt: Compared with a traditional alert pipeline, how should the Huntress default disposition differ?

choices.label: Investigate first to confirm the alert is real, then act.
choices.rationale: That's the AV/SIEM model. On Huntress the analyst has already done the "is this real" work.

choices: Read the recommendation, verify the host or user matches, execute, document, close.

choices.label: Wait for a senior to confirm every action before taking it.
choices.rationale: Wrong tempo. Most incidents don't need a senior; the recommendation is the green light.

choices.label: Forward the incident to Huntress support and wait for guidance.
choices.rationale: Misuses support. The Incident Report is the guidance.

questions.explanation: The analyst answered "is this real" for you. Your work is executing the recommendation correctly on the right entity.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a recognisable shape of second-guessing the SOC?

choices.label: Closing an incident as benign because the activity "looks legitimate" to you, with no new signal.
choices.rationale: This is exactly the cardinal mistake — unilateral disposition change without new information.

choices: Replying to the analyst with a factual clarifying question because the recommendation didn't fit the customer context.

choices.label: Doing part of the recommendation and skipping the part that worried you.
choices.rationale: Softening the recommendation — same family of mistake.

choices.label: Reopening a closed incident "to take another look" with no new signal.
choices.rationale: Re-opening on unease alone is second-guessing in slow motion.

questions.explanation: Clarifying replies are the correct surface for doubt — they're welcomed, not discouraged. The rule is about unilateral override, not disagreement.

questions.prompt: Which of these qualifies as "new signal" — a legitimate reason to challenge a closed Incident Report?

choices.label: Your gut says the analyst's disposition feels wrong.
choices.rationale: Explicitly NOT new signal. Unease alone is not information the analyst didn't have.

choices: A second host in the same tenant now shows the same process.

choices.label: You read the Evidence section again and it still looks suspicious to you.
choices.rationale: Re-reading the same Evidence doesn't add information. It produces the same conclusion the analyst weighed.

choices.label: You found a blog post describing similar-looking activity as malicious.
choices.rationale: A third-party blog isn't new signal about this specific incident.

questions.explanation: A second host with matching activity is information the analyst didn't have when they made the original disposition.

questions.prompt: A High-severity EDR Incident Report lands. Host is the IT manager's laptop. Recommendation: approve the autoruns remediation. Evidence: scheduled task running a script at login. The IT manager phones you, says he set it up yesterday himself and it's deliberate. What do you do?

choices.label: Close the incident; the IT manager confirms it's legitimate.
choices.rationale: Override the SOC on a verbal claim from one user. The right surface is a reply to the analyst, not unilateral closure.

choices.label: Approve the recommendation; the SOC's call stands.
choices.rationale: Acts before clarifying. You'd run a remediation on a senior user's machine without letting the layer that decides weigh his new context.

choices: Reply to the analyst with the new context, keep the incident open while you wait.

choices.label: Escalate to a senior because the customer disagrees with the SOC.
choices.rationale: The right body is the analyst, not the senior. Customer disagreement on its own isn't a senior-escalation surface.

questions.explanation: The IT manager's statement is new signal; the analyst is the right body to weigh it. Neither override nor blind execute.

questions.prompt: Continuing from the IT manager scenario: the analyst replies an hour later: "Confirmed legitimate, no remediation required." What do you do next?
choices: Close the ticket on your side, document the back-and-forth, and call the IT manager to confirm.

choices.label: Approve the remediation anyway, to be safe.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Approving "to be safe" against a withdrawn recommendation is second-guessing the SOC.

choices.label: Reply with: "Are you sure? It still looks suspicious."
choices.rationale: Without new signal, that's litigating the analyst's reconsideration.

choices.label: Reopen the incident in a week to revisit.
choices.rationale: Unilateral reopen without new signal is second-guessing in slow motion.

questions.explanation: The analyst has given the disposition; your job is documentation and client comms.

questions.prompt: "The SOC is a junior, I have local context they don't." Why is this misconception dangerous?
choices: Because the SOC has more context on the broader threat surface than you do; you have more context on this specific customer. The right tool for the delta is a clarifying reply, not an override.

choices.label: Because SOC analysts have direct access to senior management and will report you.
choices.rationale: Misframes the issue as politics. The actual problem is technical.

choices.label: Because customer context is irrelevant to security decisions.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Customer context is central — the issue is the channel for using it.

choices.label: Because the analyst is always right and the tech is always wrong.
choices.rationale: Caricature of the rule. Pushing back via clarifying replies is welcomed.

questions.explanation: Both sides have asymmetric context. Replies route the delta to the layer that can weigh it; overrides assume the asymmetry runs only one way.

questions.prompt: An Incident Report's Recommendation has three actions: revoke the user's session, reset their password, and remove a malicious inbox rule. You do the first two but skip the inbox-rule removal because you "want to look at it first." What's this called?
choices: Softening the recommendation — same family as second-guessing the SOC.

choices.label: Cautious response.
choices.rationale: Misframes a defect as a virtue. "Looking at it first" without new signal is unilateral disagreement disguised as caution.

choices.label: Efficient triage.
choices.rationale: Partial-action isn't a technique — it's the recommendation getting overridden one step at a time.

choices.label: A documentation issue, nothing more.
choices.rationale: Wrong category. The omitted action is a real exposure.

questions.explanation: Doing part of the recommendation and skipping the part that worried you is the lesson's named shape of second-guessing.

questions.prompt: You disagree with a recommendation. What's the right surface for that disagreement?

choices.label: Close the incident with a note explaining why you disagreed.
choices.rationale: Unilateral closure on disagreement is the cardinal mistake.

choices: A reply to the analyst on the Incident Report, phrased as a factual clarifying question, not as an argument.

choices.label: A Slack message to your senior asking them to override the analyst.
choices.rationale: Routes around the right body.

choices.label: Reopening the incident yourself to force a re-look.
choices.rationale: Unilateral reopen without new signal is second-guessing in slow motion.

questions.explanation: The analyst issued the recommendation; the reply goes to them, framed factually.

questions.prompt: A senior who asks "did you action it?" rather than "did you investigate it?" is using vocabulary that maps to the SOC model — the work is actioning the recommendation, not investigating whether it's real.
questions.explanation: The verb is deliberate. Acting on the recommendation is the work; investigating from your seat is duplicating the analyst's with worse tooling.
